beats.narration_text: Konnichiwa — this is Liam, in for Bear. Ask Claude to build an MCP server for any external API. The mcp-builder skill fires. It does not start with code. It starts with research: study the MCP protocol, pick your language — TypeScript is recommended — and plan your tools before writing a line. Let me show you the four phases and what separates a good MCP server from a working one.

props.command: Build an MCP server for the GitHub API. Cover issues, pull requests, and repository operations.
output: Phase 1: researching MCP protocol + GitHub API endpoints
Phase 2: implementing tools with naming, annotations, outputSchema

beats.narration_text: The skill runs four phases. Phase one: research. Study the MCP protocol spec, pick your language — TypeScript with streamable HTTP for remote servers, stdio for local — and plan your tool list before writing code. Phase two: implement. Set up the project structure, build your API client, then implement each tool with a Zod input schema, a clear description, and structured output. Phase three: review and test with the MCP Inspector. Phase four: create ten evaluation questions — independent, read-only, complex, realistic, and verifiable. That evaluation set is what proves the server works for real tasks, not just for its own tests.

props.sparkLine: Research first. Code second. Evaluate third.

beats.narration_text: Every tool in a well-built MCP server has four elements. First: the input schema, defined with Zod in TypeScript or Pydantic in Python — typed, constrained, with descriptions and examples on each field. Second: the output schema — define structured content using outputSchema so clients know what to expect. Third: annotations — readOnlyHint, destructiveHint, idempotentHint, openWorldHint — which tell agents how to use the tool safely. Fourth: actionable error messages that name the problem and suggest the fix. Naming convention: consistent prefix plus action, like github underscore create underscore issue or github underscore list underscore repos. And tool descriptions should be concise — one sentence of what it does, then parameters and return type.

props.sparkLine: Name it clearly. Type it strictly. Describe it once.

beats.act: teardown — the quality bar + what it gets right/bites
beats.narration_text: Here is the teardown. The architectural insight of mcp-builder is that tool quality is measured by how well it enables real-world task completion — not by how many endpoints you covered. What the skill gets right: it mandates comprehensive API coverage over workflow shortcuts, enforces TypeScript plus Zod as the recommended stack, and requires ten real evaluation questions that prove agents can actually use the server. Where it bites: the skill relies on web-fetching live SDK docs during Phase 1 — if those URLs are unavailable or stale, the research phase stalls. Evaluation questions must be read-only and verifiable, which rules out many naturally complex agent tasks. And TypeScript is the recommended language even when the team's expertise is in Python.

props.sparkLine: Tool quality = real task completion. Not endpoint count.

beats.narration_text: Verdict. The mcp-builder skill is a four-phase MCP server development guide: research, implement, review, evaluate. TypeScript plus Zod is the recommended stack; streamable HTTP for remote servers, stdio for local. Tool design rules: consistent prefix naming, typed Zod schemas, outputSchema with structuredContent, and actionable error messages. The evaluation gate requires ten independent, read-only, complex, realistic questions in XML format — that is the proof that the server enables real agent tasks, not just syntactically correct code.

artifactLines: 4 phases: research → implement → review → evaluate (in that order, no skipping)
Stack: TypeScript + Zod (recommended); Python/Pydantic as alternative
Transport: streamable HTTP (remote, stateless JSON) or stdio (local)
Tool anatomy: Zod schema + outputSchema + 4 annotations + actionable errors
Eval gate: 10 read-only, complex, verifiable questions in XML format

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Open a Claude session. Paste this: Build an MCP server for the OpenWeatherMap API. Use TypeScript and Zod. Cover current weather, forecasts, and historical data. Follow the mcp-builder skill. When Claude responds, watch whether it starts with research — loading the MCP protocol spec and the TypeScript SDK — before writing any implementation. Then check whether each tool has a Zod input schema, an outputSchema, and the four annotations: readOnlyHint, destructiveHint, idempotentHint, openWorldHint. If Claude starts coding without reading the SDK or skips the evaluation phase at the end, the skill's phase gates were not followed. That is the gate.

props.command: Build an MCP server for the OpenWeatherMap API. TypeScript + Zod. Cover current weather, forecasts, historical data. Follow the mcp-builder skill.
props.runningText: Watch: does Claude start with research (MCP spec + SDK) before coding?
output: Expected: Phase 1 research before any implementation code
Expected: each tool has Zod schema + outputSchema + 4 annotations
Red flag: coding before SDK research, or missing evaluation phase

beats.narration_text: MCP Builder. Liam, in for Bear.
